meta.title: 3.4 useContext: Data Without Drilling
meta.description: Some data needs to be available everywhere. useContext is React's way of sharing it.

blocks.prompt: Think about a web app with a dark mode toggle in the settings page. Every single component in the app -- buttons, cards, headers, footers -- needs to know whether dark mode is on. How do you think that information gets to every component? Do you think the parent passes a `darkMode` prop through every single level of nesting?

blocks.question: If a component uses `useContext(ThemeContext)` to get the current theme, does it also need to receive theme as a prop from its parent?
blocks.answer: No. That is the whole point of context -- it replaces the need for the parent to pass the data as a prop. The component reads the theme directly from the nearest ThemeContext.Provider above it in the tree, bypassing all intermediate components. If a component uses useContext for a value, it does not need that value as a prop.

options: A) An avatar image and the text "Alice"
B) Nothing -- the component returns null
C) An error because user is not passed as a prop
D) The badge div but with no image or name
problems.explanation: The component reads the `user` from AuthContext. Since the user is logged in as Alice, `user` is not null, so the component renders the badge with Alice's avatar and name. The `if (!user) return null` line means the badge is hidden when no one is logged in. No prop is needed because context provides the data.

options: A) ActionButton never reads the theme from context -- it always uses the hardcoded class `btn-light`. It should use `useContext(ThemeContext)` and set the className based on the theme value.
B) The ThemeContext.Provider is in the wrong location.
C) The setTheme function does not work.
D) ActionButton needs theme as a prop.
problems.explanation: The ActionButton component has a hardcoded className of `btn-light`. It never calls `useContext(ThemeContext)` to read the current theme. Even though the theme context exists and is correctly provided at the App level, this particular component ignores it. The fix is to add `const theme = useContext(ThemeContext)` and use it in the className: `className={theme === 'dark' ? 'btn-dark' : 'btn-light'}`.

options: A) Two bugs: (1) The CartProvider does not include `setItems` in the context value, so consumers cannot update the cart. (2) ProductPage mutates the array with `items.push` instead of using a setter function.
B) The CartProvider should use useReducer instead of useState.
C) The Header component needs to subscribe to changes separately.
D) The product page should import setItems directly from the provider file.
problems.explanation: The provider only passes `{ items }` but not the setter function. ProductPage has no way to properly update the cart. Even worse, it tries to mutate the array directly with `items.push(product)`, which React will not detect. The fix: (1) Change the provider value to `{ items, setItems }`. (2) Change ProductPage to destructure `setItems` and use `setItems(prev => [...prev, product])`. You would tell the AI: "Include setItems in the CartContext.Provider value, and update addToCart to use setItems with a functional update instead of mutating the array with push."

blocks.prompt: Explain the prop drilling problem in your own words. Then describe the three pieces of React Context (create, provide, consume) and how they solve it. What are the three most common things you see shared through context?

blocks.content: Context solves prop drilling by letting any component read shared data without it being passed through every intermediate parent. The three pieces are createContext, Provider, and useContext. When you see useContext in code, the context name tells you what data is being shared -- theme, auth, language, or some other app-wide value.

blocks.prompt: Think about the web apps you use. What data is the same across every page -- your login status, your preferred language, the color theme? All of that is likely managed through context. When you see useContext in code, you now know: this component is reading something shared across the whole app.

reviewQuestions.question: What problem does useContext solve?
options: It makes components render faster
It lets components read shared data without prop drilling through every intermediate parent
It replaces useState for managing local state
reviewQuestions.explanation: useContext lets any component in the tree read shared data directly from a Provider, without the data being passed as props through every level in between.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly! Context is like a loudspeaker -- announce the data once and any component can hear it directly.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: useContext solves prop drilling. Instead of passing data through every intermediate component, any descendant can read the value directly from the Provider.

reviewQuestions.question: What are the three pieces of the React Context system?
options: useState, useEffect, useContext
createContext, Provider, useContext
import, export, render
reviewQuestions.explanation: The three pieces are: createContext (define it), Provider (supply the value), and useContext (read the value in any descendant component).
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Great recall! Create it, provide it, consume it -- those are the three steps of Context.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The three pieces are createContext (to define it), Provider (to supply the value to the tree), and useContext (to read the value from any nested component).

reviewQuestions.question: Which of these is a common use case for React Context?
options: Passing a single prop from a parent to one child
Sharing theme, authentication, or language data across many components
Sorting a list of items alphabetically
reviewQuestions.explanation: Context is best for data that many components throughout the app need, like the current theme, logged-in user, or selected language.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right! Theme, auth, and language are the classic trio of context use cases.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Context shines when data is needed widely across the app -- things like dark mode, who's logged in, or the chosen language. For single parent-child data, props are still the way to go.
